vitals.location: The Kallang (formerly Singapore Sports Hub), Singapore
vitals.date_specific: Sportive Ride: 10 May 2026, 5:00 a.m. start; overall event ran 9-10 May 2026. Next edition not yet announced.
vitals.field_size: ~7,000 event-wide including virtual categories; 5,600+ in the three physical mass categories; 2,200+ specifically in the Sportive Ride
vitals.entry_fee: S$89 early bird / S$103 regular (10% OCBC-card discount available)
vitals.feed_zones: Not independently verified in current sources

route_options.name: Brompton World Championship (800m circuit, up to 5 laps)

climate.description: Early May in Singapore: hot, humid, tropical — a pre-dawn start is used to beat the worst of the heat.
challenges: Tropical heat and humidity even at a 5:00am start

terrain.surface: Fully paved, closed-road urban and highway

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 57. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 56. SOL REVIEW FLAG (2026-07-24, not applied — numeric dimensions locked per wave-10 rule): reviewer notes distance=2 is generous for a 40km ride (rubric implies 1), and organization/scenic_experience/community_culture/logistics/value at 3-4 are not clearly evidenced by the research dump for this format. Flagging for a human rescoring pass, not auto-applying.

course_description.character: A fully paved, urban/highway-style closed-road ride through central Singapore, not a climbing gran fondo. The 2026 Sportive route ran through the CBD, over Benjamin Sheares Bridge, past the Singapore Flyer, along West Coast Highway and the Port of Singapore, finishing inside the National Stadium at The Kallang. The draw is riding wide city roads and elevated highway/viaduct sections with zero motor traffic — something you can't do any other day of the year.
course_description.signature_challenge: Benjamin Sheares Bridge and the elevated highway on- and off-ramps — the closest thing to sustained effort on an otherwise flat-to-rolling course. Managing thousands of mixed-ability riders on shared closed roads is arguably a bigger factor than the terrain itself.
suffering_zones: Benjamin Sheares Bridge — the course's main elevation change, on a route with no verified total ascent published
Dense mass-start traffic among 2,200+ Sportive riders in a closed-road but crowded field

logistics.lodging_strategy: Hotels near The Kallang (National Stadium precinct) or Orchard Road put you close to the start/finish and well served by MRT.
logistics.transport: Start/finish at The Kallang (National Stadium precinct), central Singapore, well served by MRT.

history.origin_story: Inaugural event held 22 February 2009, starting from the F1 Pit Building with 5,400 cyclists, organized by Spectrum Worldwide with OCBC Bank as title sponsor. Grew to 9,100 riders in 2010 and 11,500 in 2014. OCBC changed organizer and relocated the event to Singapore Sports Hub in 2015. For 2026, OCBC itself owns the event with PICH Event Management as organizer, sanctioned by the Singapore Cycling Federation and supported by Sport Singapore.
notable_moments: 2014: a 24-year-old participant died from head injuries crashing during the then 59km Super Challenge category — the following edition added roughly S$300,000 in safety measures (experienced safety riders) and eliminated the 59km category

2025: the physical event was converted to virtual-only after Singapore's general election was scheduled for the same weekend, since security resources for road closures were unavailable — riders got full refunds but overseas participants publicly criticized non-refundable travel costs and the late notice
2026: the physical event returned and sold out
history.reputation: One of Southeast Asia's longest-running mass-participation cycling events. It added real safety measures after a 2014 fatality, and its 2025 physical edition was converted to virtual-only when it collided with Singapore's election date — an externally triggered scheduling conflict, not a safety incident, but one that left overseas riders with unrecovered travel costs.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Real Mass-Participation Institution, With Two Documented Incidents on Record
biased_opinion.summary: OCBC Cycle Singapore is a legitimate, long-running (since 2009) mass-participation event, not a marquee climbing gran fondo — its entire appeal is riding Singapore's CBD, Benjamin Sheares Bridge and highway viaducts on fully closed roads, something impossible on any other day. It's well-organized today, in large part because it had to become well-organized: a 2014 fatality in the (since-eliminated) 59km Super Challenge category exposed Singapore's lack of standardized mass-event safety rules and forced roughly S$300,000 in new safety measures. More recently, the 2025 physical edition was converted to virtual-only on short notice when it collided with Singapore's general election date, leaving overseas riders with sunk travel costs even after full entry refunds. Both incidents are on the public record, not rumor: the 2014 fatality directly drove the post-2014 safety changes, while the 2025 conversion to virtual-only was an externally triggered scheduling conflict, not a safety failure.
strengths: Genuinely rare access — closed CBD roads, Benjamin Sheares Bridge, and highway viaducts you cannot ride any other day
Long, real track record since 2009, now backed directly by OCBC as event owner and sanctioned by the Singapore Cycling Federation
Real post-2014 safety investment (added safety riders, eliminated the highest-risk category) following a documented fatality
Multiple entry categories (40km Sportive, 40km Foldie, 20km Straits Times Ride, Brompton World Championship, virtual options) for very different rider profiles
weaknesses: Flat-to-rolling urban course with no verified elevation total — this is a closed-road experience event, not a climbing test
A documented 2014 participant fatality during the event's former 59km category prompted the safety changes now in place

2025 physical event was cancelled on short notice due to an election-date conflict — legitimate criticism centers on the late notice and overseas riders' non-refundable travel costs, not a safety lapse
Field-size marketing ("~7,000 cyclists") blends physical and virtual categories — only ~2,200 actually rode the Sportive course itself
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A genuinely rare once-a-year closed-road ride through central Singapore, with safety measures added after a real 2014 incident — just don't expect a climbing challenge, and build in flexibility if traveling internationally given the 2025 scheduling-conflict precedent.

final_verdict.one_liner: Singapore's closed-CBD mass ride — flat, added real safety measures after a 2014 incident, once hit by an election-date scheduling conflict.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes, for the novelty of closed-road riding through central Singapore and over Benjamin Sheares Bridge — go in expecting a flat urban experience ride, not a climbing gran fondo, and if traveling internationally, build in flexibility given the 2025 scheduling-conflict precedent.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similarly urban, closed-road mass-participation ride elsewhere in Asia, compare against other national capital city rides in this database. For actual climbing in the region, look further afield.

rider_intel.search_text: No rider intelligence could be extracted from the available transcripts for the OCBC Cycle Singapore race. The transcripts provided do not contain relevant information about this specific cycling event, course conditions, or rider experiences at the Singapore Sports Hub venue. The available content discusses unrelated topics including ship transportation and general bicycle comparisons, rather than race-specific intelligence that would be useful for riders preparing for this 25-mile cycling event in Singapore.

eligibility.notes: 2026 edition ran 9-10 May 2026 (Sportive Ride on May 10), sold out with registration closed 27 April. Next edition date not yet announced. The 2025 physical edition was cancelled in favor of virtual-only rides due to a scheduling conflict with Singapore's general election, then returned to a physical format in 2026.

catalog_flags.taxonomy_note: This is a 40km, flat, noncompetitive closed-road mass-participation ride (multiple distance/format options, no overall classification on the Sportive category) rather than a standard climbing gran fondo. Flagging alongside this wave's other taxonomy calls (bordeaux-paris-ultra-cycling, korea-epic-ride) for a human review of whether format-mismatched entries like this belong in the gran_fondo tier as currently scored; discipline enum left unchanged.
